MSDM5002 HW4 Solution¶

Put all your answers to programming questions in ONE .ipynb or .py file. Answers to each question should be separated in different cells (For .py, use #%% as separator so that spyder will split them into cells). If the question reqests for other files output e.g. picture/animation, you may upload them separately.

Different questions are supposed to be unrelated and your functions should be able to run independently.

Q1¶

"Coin Toss Rainbow" is a popular carnival game. The player tosses a coin onto a canvas with a rainbow pattern and win if the coin lies within the rainbow and does not touch any of the boundaries. In this question we want to write a function to play this game.

(Part a, 2 marks)¶

Below is the canvas we are going to use for the game. Write a function draw_rainbow() that draw this canvas with matplotlib.

  • The numbers are the dimensions of the canvas and the rainbow pattern.
  • The colours on the rainbow are #FF3333, #FFFF33, #00994D, #3399FF respectively.
  • Outline the boundaries of the rainbow and the white canvas with black (#000000) and line-width 1.
  • Hide the axes ticks. Keep the axes ratio as 1:1.

(Part b, 2 marks)¶

We are using a coin with radius = 1. Assume that the coin will always fall completely inside the 28x16 canvas. Write a function toss_result(x,y) to check if the coin land on a particular colour, where the inputs x/y are the x-y coordinate of the coin's center on the canvas. The function should output a text message:

  • If it falls completely onto 1 of the coloured area, writes the colour's name (Red/Yellow/Green/Blue).
  • If it touches a black line or falls completely on the white area, writes "Failed".

Hint: First use (0,0) as the semicircles' centers to simplify the check.

(Part c, 2 marks)¶

Use your functions in part a and b to build a new function toss_rainbow(). When the function is called, it simulates one (uniformly) random toss which always falls completely in the canvas. The output of the function should be a plot of the rainbow canvas with a coin at its landing position. On the upper right hand corner of the canvas, show the text message output mentioned in part b.

  • Use #EEEEEE for the coin's colour and black for its boundary.
  • Use black for the text's colour. Use a font-size that will not cover the rainbow when displayed.

The function should output a figure that look like below, and the results should be randomized every time it is run.

Q2¶

(4 marks)¶

Suppose we start with an ordered array of $n$ coordinates $((x_1, y_1), (x_2,y_2),..., (x_n, y_n))$, where the $x_i, y_i$ are numbers generated randomly. At each iteration, we update the array of coordinates to be the midpoints between two neighbouring coordinates in the previous iteration, i.e. at the $k^\text{th}$ iteration, new coordinates are computed as:

$$ \left(x_i^{(k+1)}, y_i^{(k+1)}\right) = \left(\frac{x_i^{(k)}+x_{i+1}^{(k)}}{2}, \frac{y_i^{(k)}+y_{i+1}^{(k)}}{2}\right) $$

and the last coordinate is taken as the midpoint between the first and the last point in the previous iteration,

$$ \left(x_n^{(k+1)}, y_n^{(k+1)}\right) = \left(\frac{x_n^{(k)}+x_{1}^{(k)}}{2}, \frac{y_n^{(k)}+y_{1}^{(k)}}{2}\right) $$

Use $n=50$ to create an animation of the iteration. In the figure, you should join the last point back to the first one to make it a closed polygon. Use these settings for the animation:

  • Markers: Blue (#0000FF) circles with black (#000000) edges. Size = 5.
  • Lines between markers: Black, dotted lines with linewidth = 1.
  • Axes: Ratio should remain 1:1. Do not show the ticks.
  • Frame rate: at most 5 iterations per frame.
  • The line should be black with linewidth = 1, and the markers should be blue with marker size at least 5.
  • The axis should be hidden.
  • Note that the polygon's size will keep shrinking through the iterations. In order to make the points clear, in every frame you should center on the polygon and change the scale of the axes. The ratio of the scale on both axes should remain 1:1 through the whole animation.

Q3¶

(4 marks)¶

In a pixel-based representation, a 2D image is divided into a regular grid of pixels to store the image's colour information at different positions. Similarly, we can divide a 3D space into a regular grid of "voxel" to store the colour information at each position in the space. Voxels have been widely used in a lot of games and art works and we can use the voxel plot in matplotlib to create our own simple voxel art piece.

Create a house made by voxels like below. The colours used are:

  • Walls: #DDDDDD
  • Pillars and door: #9e6a08
  • Roof and the stairs under the door: #f2e0bd
  • Window: face color = #FFFFFF, edge color = #b6faf8, with alpha = 0.5 and line width = 1

The back side and interior of the house does not matter (Although you can make them if you like). You will get full mark as long as your plot looks similar.

Note: In a voxel plot, the image will look bright only if you rotate to a view angle such that (0,0,0) is pointing towards you, or else the colour will look very strange. For the below picture I have set the view angle to be (10, -150).

Q4¶

(Part a, 4 marks)¶

Create an animation of the nostalgic bouncing DVD logo like below. You can find the image of the logo in the attachment.

  • The logo's initial position can be anywhere, but it always travels in the 45 degree directions.
  • The figure's ratio is 4:3 (like in old television). Axes ticks should be hidden.
  • The background should be black.
  • Make the animation long enough such that the logo bounces on the sides for at least 5 times.

You should downsize the image (by your code, not external tools) before plotting, or it takes long time to process.

(Part b, 2 marks)¶

By modifying or rewrite your code in part a, make the logo change colour when it bounces to a side. The colour needs to be randomly picken from the HSV space with satuation and value equal to 1.
